items.title: Understanding Infertility Prevalence and Fecundability Decline with Age
items.content: Infertility affects approximately 17.5% of couples globally, remaining stable over recent decades. The ability to conceive declines significantly with age in women, impacting fertility planning.
keyPoints: Infertility prevalence is ~17.5% worldwide.
Fecundability rate decreases notably after age 35: reduced by 14% at ages 34–35, 19% at ages 36–37, 53% at ages 40–41, and 59% at ages 42–44.
85% of couples achieve pregnancy within 12 months; 95% within 24 months.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · Infertility and Contraception | INFERTILITY | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Etiology of Infertility: Distribution of Causes Between Genders
items.content: The causes of infertility are categorized into female factors, male factors, and unexplained cases. Female factors account for 30-40% of infertility cases, while male factors account for 40-50%. Both partners may contribute to the condition in 20-30% of cases.
keyPoints: Female causes: tubal factors, uterine etiology, ovulatory dysfunction, endocrine dysfunction.
Male causes: anatomic and endocrine factors, sexual dysfunction, genetic factors.
Distribution: Female factors (30–40%), Male factors (40–50%), Both partners (20–30%).
Unexplained infertility occurs in up to 30% of couples.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · Infertility and Contraception | INFERTILITY | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Timing of Fertility Evaluation
items.content: Initiate fertility evaluation after 12 months or sooner based on age and risk factors.
keyPoints: Start evaluation after 1 year of unprotected intercourse for most couples, as 80–85% achieve pregnancy within this period.
For females over 35 years, begin evaluation after 6 months; for those over 40 years, start immediately.
Evaluate earlier if either partner has risk factors for infertility.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 1)

items.title: Components of Initial Fertility Evaluation
items.content: Comprehensive assessment includes history, physical exam, laboratory tests, and imaging.
keyPoints: Detailed medical history for both partners is crucial to identify risk factors.
Female partner's history should cover gynecologic, endocrine, and reproductive aspects.
Male partner's history should include reproductive tract injuries, surgeries, and fertility with prior partners.
Physical exams assess weight, BP, thyroid, breast, hyperandrogenism signs, and pelvic anatomy in females; BMI, BP, and testicular exam in males.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Tools for Assessing Fertility
items.content: Use ultrasound, ovulation assessment, HSG, ovarian reserve evaluation, and endocrine tests.
keyPoints: Ultrasound assesses uterine and adnexal abnormalities; evaluates ovarian reserve via antral follicle count.
Ovulation can be confirmed with urinary LH strips, serum progesterone, or basal body temperature rise.
HSG during the follicular phase checks fallopian tube patency and identifies intrauterine pathology.
Ovarian reserve evaluated by FSH, estradiol, AMH levels, and antral follicle count; informs treatment urgency.
Endocrine tests for irregular menses include serum TSH, prolactin, and androgens to identify causes of anovulation.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 1)

items.title: Semen Analysis Parameters and Indications for Further Evaluation
items.content: Semen analysis assesses sperm count, motility, morphology, volume, and pH. Abnormalities in these parameters increase infertility risk. WHO criteria define oligoasthenozoospermia as sperm counts <15 million/mL, motility <40%, and normal morphology <4%. Physical exam and endocrine evaluation are recommended for abnormal parameters; genetic evaluation is indicated for azoospermia or severe oligospermia (<5 million/mL).
keyPoints: Semen sample collected after 2–7 days of abstinence.
Oligoasthenozoospermia: sperm counts <15 million/mL, motility <40%, normal morphology <4%.
Physical exam and endocrine evaluation for abnormal parameters (FSH, LH, prolactin, TSH).
Genetic evaluation for azoospermia or severe oligospermia (<5 million/mL) (karyotype, Y chromosome microdeletion).
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 2) | COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Preconception Counseling for Optimizing Fertility Outcomes
items.content: Preconception counseling identifies modifiable risks to optimize pregnancy outcomes. It includes lifestyle modifications, smoking cessation, and caffeine restriction. Obesity affects fertility in both genders; smoking impacts fecundity and increases miscarriage risk. Caffeine consumption should be limited to ≤2 cups per day during attempts at conception.
keyPoints: Counseling on weight management due to obesity's impact on anovulatory cycles and sperm parameters.
Smoking cessation can reverse negative effects on fecundity, reducing miscarriage and ectopic pregnancy risks.
Limit caffeine intake to ≤2 cups per day during conception attempts.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 2) | COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Management of Tubal Factor Infertility
items.content: Tubal factor infertility, accounting for 30–35% of female infertility cases, often results from tubal obstruction due to infections. IVF is the preferred treatment as it bypasses fallopian tubes and offers high success rates. Tubal repair is generally not recommended in cases with infections or hydrosalpinx; salpingectomy can improve IVF outcomes by preventing fluid efflux.
keyPoints: Tubal factor infertility constitutes 30–35% of female infertility cases.
IVF is preferred for tubal factor infertility due to high success rates.
Salpingectomy improves pregnancy rates in subsequent IVF treatments.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 2) | COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Approach to Ovulatory Dysfunction
items.content: Endocrine conditions such as hypothyroidism and hyperprolactinemia should be treated before using ovulation induction medications. Lifestyle modifications are recommended for patients with low BMI or obesity, as weight loss in obese women can increase the likelihood of spontaneous or drug-induced ovulation.
keyPoints: Treat endocrine conditions prior to ovulation induction.
Lifestyle modifications for low BMI or obesity.
Weight loss increases likelihood of ovulation (60–80% response rate with letrozole and clomiphene citrate in PCOS).
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 2)

items.title: Management of Male Infertility
items.content: Given the high prevalence of male factor infertility (40–50%), timely evaluation and treatment are crucial. For azoospermia, further evaluation is needed to distinguish between obstructive and nonobstructive causes. IVF with ICSI is recommended for severe male factor infertility.
keyPoints: High prevalence of male factor infertility (40–50%).
Evaluate azoospermia to identify obstructive vs. nonobstructive etiology.
IVF with ICSI recommended for sperm count <5 million/mL or motility <20%.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 2)

items.title: Treatment Strategy for Unexplained Infertility
items.content: In cases of unexplained infertility, ovarian stimulation with oral medications and IUI is initially recommended. IVF may be considered after 3–6 months if no success is achieved, as it offers higher success rates.
keyPoints: Initiate ovarian stimulation with oral medications and IUI for unexplained infertility.
Consider IVF after 3–6 months of unsuccessful treatment.
IVF associated with low risk of complications; monitor to reduce ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 3) | PSYCHOLOGICAL ASPECTS OF INFERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION
items.title: Endometriosis and Infertility: Treatment Strategies
items.content: In women with endometriosis, surgical intervention for advanced stages may improve fertility outcomes. Oral ovulation induction and IUI are first-line treatments for infertility due to endometriosis alone.
keyPoints: 25–50% of infertile women have endometriosis
30–50% of women with endometriosis experience infertility
Surgical treatment of stage 3 or 4 endometriosis may improve pregnancy rates
First-line treatment includes oral ovulation induction medications and IUI
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 3) | PSYCHOLOGICAL ASPECTS OF INFERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION

items.subtopic: COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 3) | PSYCHOLOGICAL ASPECTS OF INFERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION
items.title: Contraception Usage Trends and Efficacy
items.content: The most common contraceptive methods globally include tubal sterilization, male condoms, IUDs, and the pill. Long-acting reversible contraceptives are increasingly preferred due to their high efficacy compared to daily or coitus-related methods.
keyPoints: 85% chance of pregnancy over 1 year without contraception
Most common contraceptive methods: tubal sterilization (~219 million), male condoms (208 million), IUDs (161 million), pill (150 million)
LARC agents like IUDs and implants are as effective as sterilization
Daily and coitus-related contraceptives have higher failure rates
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 3) | PSYCHOLOGICAL ASPECTS OF INFERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION

items.title: Permanent Contraception: Vasectomy and Tubal Sterilization
items.content: Vasectomy is a low-risk, outpatient procedure with a failure rate of 0.1 pregnancies per 100 women per year, requiring alternative contraception for 3 months post-procedure. Tubal sterilization has a failure rate of 0.5 pregnancies per 100 women per year and can be performed postpartum or as an interval procedure.
keyPoints: Vasectomy: <2% global decline in male sterilization, 0.1 pregnancies/100 women/year failure rate
Tubal Sterilization: Represents 23% of all methods, 0.5 pregnancies/100 women/year failure rate
Postpartum and interval procedures available for tubal sterilization
Preprocedure counseling on risks and alternatives is essential
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 1)

items.title: Hormonal Contraceptives: Mechanism and Efficacy
items.content: Combined estrogen- and progestin-containing contraceptives work by suppressing FSH, LH, and altering endometrial receptivity. They are available in various forms with similar efficacy but varying compliance rates. The overall failure rate is 8 pregnancies per 100 women per year.
keyPoints: Mechanism: Suppresses FSH, LH; alters endometrial receptivity
Forms: Oral pills, transdermal patch, vaginal ring
Failure Rate: 8 pregnancies/100 women/year
Benefits: Regulates menstrual cycles, reduces cancer risk by 50% (endometrial) and 40% (ovarian)
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 1)

items.title: Contraindications and Considerations for Hormonal Contraceptives
items.content: Hormonal contraceptives are contraindicated in smokers over 35, postpartum women, and those with thrombophilias due to increased DVT risk. Obesity is a category 2 risk where benefits outweigh risks.
keyPoints: Contraindications: Smokers >35 years, immediate postpartum, history of DVT/thrombophilia
DVT Risk: Increased with estrogen-containing contraceptives (3–15 per 10,000 women-years)
Obesity: Classified as risk category 2 by USMEC
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 1)

items.title: Effectiveness and Duration of Progestin-Only Contraceptive Methods
items.content: Progestin-only contraceptives, including pills, IUDs, implants, and injectables, offer varying effectiveness rates and durations. They are suitable for women with contraindications to estrogen.
keyPoints: Failure rate of progestin-only pills: 9 pregnancies per 100 women per year
Failure rate of progestin IUDs: 0.1 pregnancies per 100 women per year
Progestin IUD effectiveness duration: 3–7 years
Injectable progesterone (medroxyprogesterone acetate) failure rate: 3 pregnancies per 100 women per year; administered every 3 months
Subdermal implant (etonogestrel) failure rate: 0.05 pregnancies per 100 women per year; lasts up to 5 years
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 2)

items.title: Nonhormonal IUDs and Their Characteristics
items.content: The nonhormonal copper IUD is a long-acting, effective contraceptive method with specific considerations regarding side effects.
keyPoints: Copper IUD failure rate: <1 pregnancy per 100 women per year
Effective duration of copper IUD: up to 12 years
Increased risk of heavy vaginal bleeding and dysmenorrhea with copper IUDs
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 2)

items.title: Lactational Contraception as a Postpartum Option
items.content: Exclusive breastfeeding can serve as an effective contraceptive method during the first 6 months postpartum due to hormonal suppression.
keyPoints: Failure rate of lactational contraception: 0.5–1.5 pregnancies per 100 women per year
Effective if menstrual cycles have not resumed and breastfeeding is exclusive
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 2)

items.title: Efficacy and Timing of Emergency Contraception
items.content: Emergency contraception is most effective when administered promptly after unprotected intercourse, especially within the 5 days following. The copper IUD is highly effective if inserted within this timeframe, while ulipristal acetate offers superior efficacy among oral options.
keyPoints: Probability of pregnancy post-unprotected intercourse: 8% generally, up to 30% near ovulation.
Copper IUD insertion within 5 days is the most effective emergency contraception method.
Ulipristal acetate has a failure rate of 1-3%, making it more effective than other hormonal options like levonorgestrel.
Timing of administration is crucial, particularly within 3 days prior to ovulation for highest efficacy.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 3)

items.title: Emergency Contraception Options and Considerations
items.content: Various emergency contraception options are available including the copper IUD, ulipristal acetate, levonorgestrel, and combined hormonal pills. The choice depends on timing post-intercourse, patient weight, and side effect profile.
keyPoints: Copper IUD prevents fertilization and implantation; effective for obese women where hormonal methods may fail.
Ulipristal acetate (30 mg) is administered up to 5 days after intercourse and inhibits the LH surge.
Levonorgestrel delays ovulation with fewer side effects than combined pills.
Emergency contraception should not be withheld based on body weight; offer promptly without pregnancy tests.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 3)

items.title: Importance of Patient-Centric Contraception Counseling
items.content: Contraceptive counseling should be patient-centric, focusing on individual needs and future pregnancy plans. Providers must discuss various contraception methods, their side effects, noncontraceptive benefits, efficacy, compliance requirements, and impact on future fertility.
keyPoints: Discuss different methods of contraception, side effects, noncontraceptive benefits, efficacy, compliance, and impact on future fertility.
Facilitate patient-centric care by discussing plans for future pregnancy and whether childbearing is complete.
Review detailed patient history to identify contraindications such as migraines with aura, smoking, and hypertension.
Refer to the most updated USMEC or WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use guidelines when counseling patients with comorbidities.
Adopt a shared decision-making approach where the patient's choice is guiding and discussions are nonjudgmental.
Offer adolescents access to the full range of contraceptive options.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · CONTRACEPTION COUNSELING

items.title: Prescribing Hormonal Contraceptives in Low-Risk Patients
items.content: In low-risk patients, hormonal contraceptives can be prescribed from menarche to menopause. However, it is crucial to regularly evaluate side effects and assess any changes in the patient's medical history.
keyPoints: Hormonal contraceptives can be prescribed from menarche to menopause in low-risk patients.
Regular evaluation of side effects is necessary.
Assess for changes in the patient's medical history.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · CONTRACEPTION COUNSELING

items.explanation: The prevalence of infertility is ~17.5% globally.

items.explanation: Both male and female factors are identified in 20–30% of couples with infertility.

items.explanation: An HSG is performed to assess tubal patency.

items.question: Which of the following markers does NOT predict the likelihood of pregnancy and live birth?
items.explanation: Markers like AMH, antral follicle counts, and serum FSH do not predict pregnancy likelihood.

items.explanation: Evaluation should be started immediately if the woman is over 40.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 1)

items.explanation: Oligoasthenozoospermia is defined by sperm counts <15 million/mL.

items.question: What should be performed in patients with azoospermia or severe oligospermia (<5 million/mL)?
items.explanation: Patients with azoospermia or severe oligospermia should have a genetic evaluation.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT indicated as part of the initial workup of infertility?
items.explanation: Semen analysis is part of the initial evaluation, while diagnostic laparoscopy, endometrial biopsy, and thrombophilia testing are not.

items.question: What is the recommended caffeine consumption limit for women attempting pregnancy?
items.explanation: Women should be counseled to restrict caffeine consumption to ≤2 cups while attempting pregnancy.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 2) | COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which medication is recommended as the first-line treatment for anovulatory infertility due to PCOS?
items.explanation: Letrozole is recommended as the first-line treatment for anovulatory infertility due to PCOS.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 2)

items.question: What should be done prior to using ovulation induction medications in patients with endocrine conditions?
items.explanation: Endocrine conditions such as hypothyroidism and hyperprolactinemia should be treated prior to using ovulation induction medications.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 2)

items.question: In men with obstructive azoospermia, how can sperm be procured?
options: Direct aspiration from the epididymis or testis
IVF with ICSI using donor sperm
items.explanation: In men with obstructive azoospermia, sperm can be procured by direct aspiration from the epididymis or testis.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 2)

items.question: What is a recommended treatment for male sexual dysfunction to address infertility?
items.explanation: Treatment of male sexual dysfunction includes avoidance of exogenous androgens to address infertility.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 3) | PSYCHOLOGICAL ASPECTS OF INFERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION
items.question: Which treatment is recommended as a first-line approach for infertility associated with endometriosis alone?
options: Surgical removal of endometriotic lesions in stage 1 or 2 endometriosis
Use of oral ovulation induction medications and IUI
Prolonged medical management to suppress endometriotic lesions
items.explanation: First-line treatment for infertility associated with endometriosis alone includes use of oral ovulation induction medications and IUI.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 3) | PSYCHOLOGICAL ASPECTS OF INFERTILITY | CONTRACEPTION

items.explanation: The contraceptive patch is associated with lower efficacy in women weighing more than 90 kg.

items.question: What is the overall failure rate for combined hormonal contraceptives?
items.explanation: The overall failure rate for combined hormonal contraceptives is 8 pregnancies per 100 women per year.

items.question: Which of the following is a benefit of hormonal contraceptives?

items.question: Which progestin-only contraceptive has the lowest failure rate per 100 women per year?

items.question: What is a common side effect associated with progestin-only hormonal contraception?
items.explanation: Common side effects of progestin-only hormonal contraception include irregular bleeding, acne, breast tenderness, and pain.

items.question: Which contraceptive method is associated with a failure rate of <1 pregnancy per 100 women per year for up to 12 years?

items.question: What is the typical duration of effectiveness for the subdermal etonogestrel implant?

items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 2)

items.question: Which method of emergency contraception is most effective if inserted within 5 days of unprotected intercourse?
items.explanation: The copper IUD is the most effective choice for emergency contraception if inserted within 5 days of unprotected intercourse.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 3)

items.question: What is the primary mechanism by which ulipristal acetate prevents pregnancy?
options: Blocks progesterone receptors and inhibits ovulation
items.explanation: Ulipristal acetate blocks the ability of endogenous progesterone to act on its receptors, inhibiting the LH surge and delaying or inhibiting ovulation.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 3)

items.question: Why should emergency contraception not be delayed for a pregnancy test or clinical examination?
items.explanation: Emergency contraception should be offered promptly because its efficacy decreases as time passes from the unprotected intercourse.

items.statement: Hormonal contraceptives are associated with significant weight gain.

items.statement: Barrier methods of contraception have higher effectiveness compared to LARC methods.
items.explanation: Barrier methods have lower effectiveness due to inconsistent and incorrect use, compared to the high effectiveness of LARCs.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 2)

items.statement: The failure rate for all hormonal emergency contraception is approximately 1-3%.
items.explanation: The passage states that the overall failure rate for hormonal emergency contraception ranges from 1 to 3%.

items.explanation: The passage indicates that treatment should not be withheld from overweight and obese women, even though body weight can affect efficacy.

items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · CONTRACEPTION COUNSELING

items.statement: Hormonal contraceptives can be prescribed from menarche to menopause in low-risk patients.
items.explanation: In a low-risk patient, hormonal contraceptives can indeed be prescribed from menarche to menopause.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · CONTRACEPTION COUNSELING

items.assertion: Ovulation can be assessed by measuring serum progesterone levels.
items.reason: Serum progesterone is measured 7 days after ovulation to confirm it.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Pregnancy rates are higher after an HSG test compared to no testing.
items.reason: This is likely due to tubal flushing of mucus plugs during the procedure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Advanced maternal age is associated with a higher risk of aneuploidy.
items.reason: Advanced paternal age (>40 years) is associated with adverse health outcomes in offspring.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 2) | COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 1)

items.assertion: IVF offers the highest success rates for couples with tubal factor infertility.
items.reason: Tubal repair or reconstruction is typically not recommended due to low success rates and increased risk of ectopic pregnancy.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why IVF is preferred over tubal repair.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · FERTILITY EVALUATION (part 2) | COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 1)

items.assertion: In women with diminished ovarian reserve, treatment is escalated to IVF.
items.reason: The overall live birth rates are lower with ovulation induction and IUI in these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the escalation to IVF.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 2)

items.assertion: Men with severe male factor infertility should be treated with IUI alone.
items.reason: Severe male factor infertility is characterized by a sperm count <5 million/mL or motility <20%.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; IVF with intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) is recommended for severe male factor infertility.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · COUNSELING AND TREATMENT (part 2)

items.assertion: Hormonal contraceptives reduce the risk of ovarian cancer.
items.reason: They provide a 40% reduction in the risk of ovarian cancer.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 1)

items.assertion: Routine screening for familial thrombotic disorders is recommended before prescribing hormonal contraceptives.
items.reason: Hormonal contraceptives are associated with a slightly increased risk of deep-vein thrombosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; routine screening is not recommended despite the slight increase in DVT risk.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 1)

items.assertion: The copper IUD is a nonhormonal contraceptive method.
items.reason: It works as a spermicidal without containing hormones.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the copper IUD is considered nonhormonal.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 2)

items.assertion: The failure rate of male condoms is higher than that of progestin-only pills.
items.reason: Male condoms have a failure rate of 17–21 pregnancies per 100 women per year, while progestin-only pills have a failure rate of 9 pregnancies per 100 women per year.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why male condoms have a higher failure rate than progestin-only pills.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · TYPES OF CONTRACEPTION (part 2)

items.assertion: Providers should review detailed patient history to identify potential contraindications.
items.reason: This is necessary to ensure the safety and appropriateness of contraceptive methods for patients with conditions like migraines with aura, smoking, or hypertension.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why a detailed patient history should be reviewed.
items.reference: Infertility and Contraception · CONTRACEPTION COUNSELING
